headline: Yellowstone County Created, 1883
body:
Well now, if you ever wanted to see a town spring up faster than a jackrabbit on a hot griddle, you ought to have been in Montana Territory back in '83. On the twenty-sixth day of February, that year, the territorial legislature, bless their industrious hearts, decided that the vast, sprawling expanse of Custer and Gallatin Counties was just too much for a body to keep track of. So, with a stroke of the legislative pen, they carved out a brand-new slice of the frontier and christened it Yellowstone County. Named, naturally, for that grand old river that cuts through the land like a silver ribbon, the Yellowstone. It was a move born of necessity, you see, for the Northern Pacific Railway was pushing its iron snake across the plains, and wherever that railway went, towns, like mushrooms after a spring rain, were bound to follow.

And follow they did. Billings, a mere babe in the woods, was designated the county seat. This wasn't some sleepy hamlet; no sir, Billings was a town with ambition, a place where the very air crackled with enterprise. It sat on the north bank of the Yellowstone, a strategic spot indeed, some 915 miles west of St. Paul, Minnesota, and perched at an elevation of 3,112 feet above the sea. The Minnesota and Montana Land and Improvement Company, a shrewd bunch, had laid out the townsite, dedicating it to Frederick Billings, a man of considerable influence with the Northern Pacific. They called it the "Magic City," and for good reason. Just a year prior, where Billings now bustled, there was naught but a carpet of verdure, as one local chronicler put it. But by 1883, it boasted a population estimated between 1,500 and 2,000 souls, all of them, one might presume, of the "law-abiding, hard-working, energetic" sort, with nary a drone among them.

The county itself was no small potatoes, stretching westward from the Big Horn River for 110 miles and northward from the Yellowstone for over fifty. It encompassed nearly 6,000 square miles, a territory almost the size of Massachusetts, and considerably larger than Connecticut and Rhode Island combined. The land was rich, with fertile valleys like the Clark's Fork bottom, and the vast coal fields of the Bull Mountains. It was a land of promise, where cattle and sheep grazed on boundless ranges, destined for shipment east. Indeed, the 1883 "Residence and Business Directory of Billings, Montana" proudly declared, "Billings is the county seat and geographical centre of Yellowstone county, and the commercial depot for a vast extent of surrounding country." This was a place where fortunes were to be made, where the raw materials of the West were gathered and sent forth to fuel the hungry markets of the East. The very air, they said, was a tonic, invigorating and pure, a cure for all manner of ailments. It was a place where the future was not merely hoped for, but actively, vigorously, and profitably built, brick by industrious brick, upon the fertile soil of a newly formed county.
